label: ai
topic: The road to implementation — challenges ahead
text:
Translating the Paris Agreement from diplomatic text into actual emissions reductions requires navigating a set of challenges that the agreement's architects were candid about not having solved. The first is the emissions gap: the nationally determined contributions submitted in advance of Paris, even if fully implemented, were estimated to produce roughly 2.7 degrees Celsius of warming, meaning the ambition ratchet embedded in the five-year review cycle must do enormous work simply to close the distance between current pledges and the 2-degree goal, let alone the 1.5-degree aspiration.

The second challenge is institutional. The Paris rulebook, the detailed technical decisions about how to measure, report, and verify emissions, how to account for forest carbon, and how to handle international carbon markets, took three more years of negotiation to complete at COP24 in Katowice, Poland, in 2018. Several contested elements remained unresolved even then, particularly the rules governing market mechanisms under Article 6, which were not finalised until COP26 in Glasgow in 2021. The complexity of these technical negotiations meant that the framework for implementing the agreement was still under construction years after the agreement itself had entered into force.

The third challenge is political continuity. The agreement binds governments that will change; the contributions submitted in 2015 reflected the priorities of administrations that may be replaced by others with different views on climate policy. The United States demonstrated in 2017 how quickly the architecture can be undermined from within by a single large party, though it also demonstrated, when the Biden administration rejoined in 2021, that re-entry is technically straightforward. The deeper question is whether the transparency and accountability mechanisms are robust enough to sustain momentum through election cycles, economic downturns, and competing crises across the decades the agreement is designed to govern.

Finally, the finance dimension remains a persistent source of friction. Developing countries entered the post-Paris period expecting the hundred-billion-dollar-per-year commitment to be met by 2020. It was not. Various accounting methodologies produced different figures, but by the most generous count the target was not reached until 2022, and much of what was counted consisted of loans rather than grants. The shortfall eroded trust at precisely the moment when trust was most needed, because the first global stocktake in 2023 confirmed what scientists had been warning: the collective trajectory of national pledges remained incompatible with the temperature goals the agreement had set. Closing the gap between aspiration and action remains the defining challenge of the post-Paris era.
